speaker: interviewer
text: Sixth. We're speaking with Sol Einhorn. Sol, we were just talking about how you arrived in Bergen-Belsen, and you were saying how awful it was. Again-- when you arrived, was there any routine of registering you? Taking care of you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: No formality or nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No formality, no registration, nothing. The only welcome that we got them in Bergen-Belsen is [? floormen. ?] We came off the train-- I don't know how many people we were. We were-- thousands. A hundred-- thousands, I would say. And no counting. No appellplatz. Just let yourself. And we had to go to through a line where there were kapos. And blockaltestes, the white stripes, the leadership from the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did that happened? At first they hold us up. They were in the wagons when we came from Harzug to Bergen-Belsen. We within ourselves, we had in the wagons, closed wagons. And we were very close. So they killed a couple of the blockaltestes and the kapos. Where they were hitting, and punishing, and they're killing people in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I am not talking about the Nazis. I'm talking now the Pollacks, and then maybe some even Jews between us. And some Germans, but they were prisoners of war. And they-- and the [INAUDIBLE]. In the-- in the-- in the-- inside locked wagons, they killed them. They beat them. And when we arrived, they told the other kapos in Bergen-Belsen, the other leaders, about what we did. I mean, I was nothing. I didn't do anything. So-- they put themselves up on the line, and it stinks, and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapos from Bergen-Belsen. A hundred or so. Kapos-- a hundred. And we had to [INAUDIBLE] to do them. And they killed-- hit them on the head, and kicked them. All from our people when they arrived to Bergen-Belsen into the other side. Until then, we were then over to the other side. I survived that. And I know, I got-- I got-- hit in the head. A mark here in the head with the stick. Not still there. And there was bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you moved-- excuse me-- from one camp to another, were these only men that were being moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men. No women. No. No. No, we never-- no contact whatsoever. Never saw any women. Only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And only the men were moved from one camp to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. As far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know if there were women's sections in those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. In the camps where I was, no women whatsoever. We never saw women in the camps. They were at different camps. And same like my sister was in camp where she died. There were different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of sexual activity in the men's camps of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was not a question from sexual activities. The question was from survival, from eating. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life. I mean, to put it in your mouth, that's water. Bergen-Belsen was water. We didn't have enough water to survive. I mean, to live on. People were dying what anything. They put me to work in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After they-- pulling dead bodies into a big grave. You know, a grave was made with a bulldozer. Huge. A couple, that they had. And I couldn't pull a body. So four of us, we, with our strength, we tied to the feet. One string to the feet. We have-- two strings to both feets. Two strings to the hand. And four of us pulled them a half a kilometer to the-- or whatever the bodies were. Tie them. Pull them over there. And just untie them. And just throw the body into the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, you picked up bodies from all over the camp? People were just dying?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over the camps, yeah. And I just pulled them. And-- now, we kept on for pulling them. The peoples just kept on dying. We kept on pulling, and people kept on dying-- day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have any idea how large the camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea how large the camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge. Huge. Huge. Then in Bergen-Belsen, they had another section on the other side. You could have seen-- far away, where was straight ground-- women. They had a women's camp at Bergen-Belsen. That's the only camp what I know of-- I mean, when I was-- where they had women in the concentration camp on the other side in that camp. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was going on for a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did other people--

speaker: interviewee
text: One night-- one night, that the hunger was so, so, terrible. So terrible. Once a day, they gave a little soup. Water was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went over that, once night. One day, in the middle of the day, we jumped over the fence to a kitchen. There were four or six of us. Just-- we saw potatoes. And one of my friends got shot in the foot. And that-- I jumped over with a few potatoes. Saved-- we have like a group. We were six of us. Six young fellas. I mean, my age. And he's in Israel now. He survived the shot. He went into the fat [INAUDIBLE] and the meat here. And jumped over. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] You can't-- speak about it. You can't talk about it. You can put it on paper what Bergen-Belsen was. Yeah. One night, I felt like-- we walked around like scavengers in the night that evening. And all of a sudden, I smelled-- smell like a-- like a dog smells. So we walked around also for four, six of us. Friends, so called. You know. And they were cooking. There were little fires. And they were cooking a potato, one place I'm at-- whatever. You know. Little fires were going. And in one place, one barrack had a little fire. That's right. And nobody-- no Gestapos inside. Only outside around the camp. And they let us-- they just-- die. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So smell very good. Smelled like meat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I say him, you know, it smells like meat. Yeah. Meat. And we go over closer to a group, a group of people there. About 10 of them, 10 or 12 guys. Go over closer. And we got beaten just to get away for there, you know. We were-- in my age, you know. So we just walked away.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, I found out that group were cannibal. They were eating-- they were eating, cooking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] human. It was a doctor from France. And there was some other two doctors. And there were some Russians. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the ones who were eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were eat-- cooking from the men-- men liver from the men. From the [INAUDIBLE], from dead bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took apart dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cooking and then-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: They cut open--

speaker: interviewee
text: They found. They cut open the bodies. They know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get those things to cut with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- a play knife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't need a surgeon knife.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but how did they even get a knife?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a knife. They had knives there. Whatever. You know, who knows? And they-- and how did I found out? I didn't know. In the evening, in the morning. [GERMAN] They called them the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: That means, cannibal.

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. And they took them, and they killed them. We weren't liberated yet. Still they-- the-- our own people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just made a [INAUDIBLE]. Took these guys-- I don't know how many of them were involved-- and killed them right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They found the bodies--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish people who would do the cannibals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so, no. No. I don't think they were-- Jewish people-- I didn't know. We didn't know. They found bodies the next day opened, taken out. And I know this is what happened. And then they killed these people what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But besides that part-- which is really horrible-- you said Bergen-Belsen was extremely, like awful. What made it different from the other camps you were in? What was going on there that made it worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one, they didn't feed. Number one-- at least in other camps, you had one meal. You had a soup or they-- you got half a piece of bread. At Bergen-Belsen, you practically get nothing. And you have no work. They didn't put you to work. And I didn't go out to a [NON-ENGLISH] to work. There was just nothing there. They put you in to die. Practically, to die the last-- the last-- of several months, or several weeks, or whatever. Just to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people just sat around all day? Nobody did anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Some of them, they put to work to clean or to schlep the bodies like I schlepped the bodies. They put me to work. Just sit around and die. And the typhus and sickness. And you saw people going not-- and not-- loosing everything. Just walking and dropping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you able to assess what gave the survivors the strength to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it had a lot to do with belief and HaShem, as far as I'm concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never discussed it with anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You never discussed it with anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- I-- after the war or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even when you were in the camp, did you talk about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp-- yeah. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Yeah, it was discussed with my friends. As long as I had my friend, Yankev Hirsch. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and God will help me. And we'll see through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever make friends with any new people that you met in the camps? Were you there long enough? Were you able to make friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that way. In a way, yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. From-- from Dyhernfurth I made friends. And we survived-- from Funfteichen I-- from Harzug. We went to Bergen-Belsen. I made friends. And we went for potatoes, and try to share it together, and so on, and so forth. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever see any of these people after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah. That's an-- oh, that's an interesting thing what happened. I'm in the clothing line all the year. It was in-- I started in the United States. And the imports, clothing, and I started small, and always made a living on it. And I had one clothing store on Grand Street on Lower East Side. And I had a couple of people working for me. I had a tailor, and I had three, four salesman. And one day, I'm at the register, and I didn't say, I'm the boss. The business was running while I had other two stores running. Running from one store to the other. And I'm the store, and a man walks in. And I hear his voice. And he's trying on a shirt, a suit with another friend. And the salesman talks to him, and I'll do this for you, and I'll do that for you, I'll make it a little shorter, I'll make fix it. Don't worry. And he makes the sale. So the one suit is going to sell him another suit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I turn around, I look at him. I know the man. I let him finish, and I go over. Aren't you Mr. Fishov? You remembered his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said the man would talk to the [GERMAN] in and Dyhernfurth. And maybe saved my life with his son to go into the work as a schlosser. A--

speaker: interviewer
text: Locksmith.

speaker: interviewee
text: A locksmith. Mr. Fishov, you know who I am here? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't say anything else. And he thought me the words. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I repeated this to him. Oy, is that you? [LAUGHS] He kissed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And said, where's Moishe? Moishe is now here in Brooklyn. He's married. He has four children. He went to [NON-ENGLISH] with us. He got [NON-ENGLISH]. And he's OK. And he, what are you doing? He was the manager for Barton's Candy. Pop man-- managing, and so on, and so forth. That was an experience here. You asked me if I met somebody from--

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have a been a very special--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then Moishe came to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he brought his friends, and I met his wife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Beautiful. That was quite an experience. And then I met another person from big camp. I want for a trip to Israel in 1960. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I looked up for survivors from the concentration camp, the list, and so on. And I asked the [NON-ENGLISH] to find out what's-- who's alive, who from the town, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one guy told me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is alive. My god. We were in [NON-ENGLISH]. We made a [INAUDIBLE], took a taxi. Went over [NON-ENGLISH]. He's married. He has children. I said, what happened in [NON-ENGLISH]. Why you want to take [iNAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day later, one day later, the Russians surrounded the camp. The wanted to burn us. Instead they shall burn us, they surrounded the camp, and they didn't get a chance. And the whole hospital got-- I mean, the people in it got liberated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my people, people that I met after the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, let's just go back a little bit deeper. Was Bergen-Belsen the last place you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then came liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English tanks came in. We didn't know. We just saw one-- the day and the evening-- that Germans are pulling out slowly. They leave only one here, one there, one there on the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guards. Only here and there are guards. You know, we didn't know what's going on. And in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] English row of tanks marched into the camp. All of them. And the captain came out. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I should say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say what you want to say. Say everything you want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: The English captain came out of the tank. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, who speaks English? Nobody spoke English. I didn't. Nobody around. We were 95% we were Jewish people from Poland, from Czechoslovakia, from Hungary, from Romania, from Lithuania, from Bulgaria, from France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody from Germany? Not from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. Very few Germans. I don't know why-- German Jews I counted. And who speaks English? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A little Polish Jew from Warsaw stood up. I speak English. He spoke a little bit of English. SO the first thing he asked him, what nationality we are. We what-- hundred thousands, thousands, hundreds of people, whatever around the tanks. So he says, Jewish. He didn't know what mean you have the yellow thing here. Jewish. So he says, Hitler let live so many Jews? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Polish Jew says, I mean the translator, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He makes believe that he's English. They want to see we're going to react. And we all moved back a couple of-- quite a few feet from the tanks and we got scared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they start talking. And then we saw it's not so. That he is English. It was just one individual--

speaker: interviewer
text: One Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summit. One individual captain in the summit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in the camps-- let me just ask you this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We hear a lot about how-- how soap was made from the human flesh. Did you ever see any of that? You don't see any of those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know if I see it. I didn't see too much soap anywhere. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't see any of the things that we hear about how-- how human parts were used to make other things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was quite a-- and-- and in Bergen-Belsen, whatever was left of the survivors-- after all the kills and all the death in that camp-- well, they blow them from all over in that camp. I believe the army, that captain, or that army whoever came in killed half of the population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: My personal belief. You'll ask me how. Any intelligent-- any man with a little bit of sense, any common sense will not go ahead and give for the population what didn't eat, and didn't drink, and didn't have the typhus, and sickness, and lice. And they're falling, they're running from them from front and from back. Walking and dying. Goes ahead and gives them that day when they came in-- a kilo [INAUDIBLE] fat meat what I had and with a whole bread. When they gave this for the population in Bergen-Belsen, who ever gave the order to give it to them, killed half of the population.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't think about what they needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Accidental or deliberately, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do not know. Well, if they wouldn't have give that, and after that it started out. Next day, you saw people falling like flies after they ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ate, too. And I got sick. And I was-- and I believe I was-- I had some friends, they survived. Kept me-- one helped the other. Made hot tea, hot water, hot whatever. I think I was under typhus a couple of days. I didn't know for myself. And if God gives, yes-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to know what was the logistics? They came into the camp, they said, you're free, right? The war was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say-- yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came and-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't come into camps, the English, and say, you're free. We knew automatically a sense that we were free. I mean, the camps were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They can't-- that means that you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where you going to go? OK. So they had to-- the trains were bombed. And they start talking to go back home. No trains, no buses, no transportation. Nothing was moving. Only walking. You could walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Red Cross? They didn't come to help?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there, I mean, in a way. I didn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- weren't they in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see-- in the camps? Before?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, at liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Bergen-Belsen? I didn't see them. Now, they did come in a week later. Or two weeks later came in organizations from different part of the world from Sweden, from the United States to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were still sitting in Bergen-Belsen then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Bergen-Belsen, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what I did there-- we went to the magazines. They had, the Germans, outside. And they have clothing. We took. We took off the striped clothing. We changed clothing with our clothing. We took sheets. They had sheets, pillowcases, and we took. I remember I went in with two of my friends to the town-- village, German village nearby. And I begged for a couple of eggs. I didn't kill. I didn't avenge. I didn't do. Eggs or a piece of bread, fresh bread, you know. And this is what I ate to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For nothing? They gave it to you for nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they known what was going on in the camps? Those Germans that gave you the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure they know. Sure they know. How couldn't they know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Nothing. A lot of them closed their doors when they saw us coming. Closed shops. Shut the doors and so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about-- I mean, look.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just were hanging around in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hanging around and trying to survive. And then they were talking about-- it started up-- by the way, after came the [NON-ENGLISH], and organization. [NON-ENGLISH], some organization. And they took people to Sweden. Sick people, typhus people to get them better. And I was sick. And they took them on the train from Bergen-Belsen, put them on the train, and the train went to Lubeck, in Germany. No far from Bergen-Belsen. In Lubeck, they had boats. And the boats took them over to Sweden. I-- I-- went ahead and I had my teeth-- my teeths were knocked out. I was only bones. I couldn't walk. Couldn't walk far. I remember, I went into that village. I couldn't make it practically. And that wasn't far. And I decided that time that I'm going to go instead to try to schlep my bag go home, and go to Sweden. In Sweden, they were talking [INAUDIBLE] they want in the war. And they try to make good for the people, what they went through the war. [INAUDIBLE] And I went on the to the train. And as soon as I came to the train to the English. They took a look at me. And I started walking out. They didn't let me walk out. I thought I hear they're holding me. They don't let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead, they brought a stretcher. And they put me on the stretcher. And I didn't walk anymore. And they put me on the stretcher on the train. They took me down from this-- from the train on the stretcher. This was the English. And they put me in the boat. In a-- like a thing, you know? Also like a stretcher there. It took only a day or a night into Goteborg. Where's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden. And [INAUDIBLE] here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody with you that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody close with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got to know some people there also. I got to know some people who were in Goteborg in the hospital. They took us to a hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On that hospital I was four or six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden in Goteborg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they took care of you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: They took care of you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they took care of me. First of all, they saved me from top to bottom. Everything. On-- you know, and scrubbed me. Nurses. And put me in a clean bed like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I have lice? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got the lices. That-- I have-- you know, you say, shaving. I forgot. In Dyhernfurth, in the concentration camp-- I reminded myself with shaving. Twice a week, they shaved my head in the middle, in case if I escape. Everybody was shaved twice a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'll know who you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to finish up this tape, and we'll come back with the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
